EXCELLENCE 1.1 Objectives and ambition (a) Objectives Natural hazards continue to cause immense harm worldwide through their direct and indirect impacts on the built environment, the economy and the overall functionality of society. In particular, direct damage, financial losses and operation interruptions caused by earthquakes and strong wind, combined with the impact of soil-structure interaction and hazards of geotechnical origin (i.e., liquefaction), are critical issues that still need to be addressed fully via innovative research and development. When considered collectively, the multi-hazard nature of their impacts, interaction and cascading effects also form a critical area requiring advanced research. Past seismic events (e.g., L’Aquila 2009, Central Italy 2016-2017, Samos Island Aegean Sea 2020, Albania 2019, Croatia 2020), just to mention the most recent ones, have had an immense impact on society and continually highlight the need for future research and innovation. For example, the 2020 Aegean Sea earthquake left approximately 15,000 people homeless and had an estimated damage in excess of €400M, not to mention the numerous casualties. Local soil conditions of the Dures coastal area played a significant role in the seismic performance of buildings during the November 2019 Earthquake in Albania, which took 52 human lives, saw more than 3,000 people were injured, 14,000 remain homeless and 14,000 buildings damaged. Geotechnical instabilities, soil liquefaction and appearances of sinkholes during the 2020 Petrinja, Croatia earthquake amplified the total damage and made the reconstruction efforts much more complicated and costly. Similarly, climate-related hazards also have the potential for notable impact on structures and infrastructures. During the past decades, strong storms have induced severe human losses and extensive damage to properties. For example, the great storm of 15th October 1987 caused €5M in damage and killed 34 people in France and the United Kingdom. The Lothar and Martin storms in December 1999 caused €13.5M of damage in Europe and killed 125 people. These impacts of storms are continually growing in terms of economic losses along with their occurrence frequency as a result of climate change. The database of the European Severe Storm Laboratory (ESSL) documents almost 60,000 cases of structural damage and collapse due to extreme winds recorded in Europe in the last 3 years, with enormous economic damage, situations of discomfort and risk for people. Many of these are associated with tornadoes and thunderstorms, the intensity of which could increase in the coming years due to global warming, leading to increasingly destructive events. According to Munich Re (GeoRisk Research Group), economic losses due to natural hazards are approximately equally distributed between earthquakes (35%), floods (30%) and windstorms (28%). In terms of fatalities, earthquakes (47%) and windstorms (45%) are predominant compared to floods (7%). While the impacts of these events are clear, they continuously reveal the need for more inspired and innovative research and technological developments in order to improve the resilience in the design of new structures/infrastructures and the increase of existing ones. For example, research into the seismic performance of non-structural elements requires further examination as they are a well-known source of economic losses, life-safety hazard for occupants and potential for downtime. This can be done through the experimental testing and evaluation of different components, along with the development of innovative solutions that can mitigate their impacts. Furthermore, the integration of risk-based concepts used in risk management and insurance sectors into a more engineer-based context like the design, assessment and practical implementation of structural solutions still has large gaps that need to be addressed. Research and innovation are required for the development and testing of novel solutions like low-cost seismic isolation systems, or a deeper understanding of the role of basin and local site effects, which tremendously increased the vulnerability and damage to some buildings during the recent Samos Island Aegean Sea (2020). Innovative materials and technologies are also required to contribute to the drive towards a more green and sustainable building and construction sector in Europe. It is clear how society is still somewhat behind in addressing important “open” issues in the design and retrofit of structures against natural hazards and more shrewdly oriented research is required to address them, whilst incorporating new technologies, methodologies and techniques developed, or currently under development, in recent years. This is not just from the perspective of direct impacts (i.e., repair costs) but also from a disruption and functionality perspective (i.e., indirect losses and downtime). The examples cited indicate the need for innovative and curiosity-driven experimental research at advanced experimental facilities available in Europe and around the world. These issues are critical to address in order to foster a next-generation and green society with increased resilience to these hazards. In this context, ERIES will pursue the following specific objectives: 1. Offer transnational access (TA) to the best European experimental testing facilities for earthquakes, wind, geotechnical engineering and structural interaction and also to key facilities in North America. 2. Integrate and build upon the successful results and implementation of past European projects like NERA, 101058684 ERIES – Part B – 5 SERIES, LIQUEFACT, SAFELAND and SERA and expand these capabilities to wind and geotechnical issues in a more multi-risk and holistic manner that aims to improve global resilience of society to natural hazards. 3. Provide authoritative guidance for several European stakeholders, ranging from Governmental and Civil Protection authorities across the continent to construction and consulting companies as well as to the code committees responsible for the development and updating of the Eurocode technical standards. 4. Develop innovative techniques and standards for experimental testing in earthquake engineering, wind engineering, geotechnical engineering and structural engineering in general through advanced laboratory synergies and capabilities not generally available worldwide. 5. Development of new and innovative techniques to improve the response of the structures to multiple hazards to result in more integrated mitigative solutions. 6. Develop innovative experimental testing methods involving the simultaneous operation of different laboratories via geographically distributed hybrid simulation to considerably increase the combined European experimental capacities and promote a more harmonized and integrated community of research infrastructures (RIs) across Europe. 7. Promote the development and experimental testing of greener and more sustainable solutions for the various problems faced when addressing seismic, wind and geotechnical hazards under the broader umbrella of climate-based risks and impacts. 8. Foster the development, through the TA projects funded, of innovative approaches in the treatment of economic losses, their direct impacts on buildings and infrastructures in addition to how issues like functionality and downtime may be incorporated. 9. Break down the sectoral division barriers that exist between the different disciplines (e.g., structural and geotechnical engineering) and promote a more multi-disciplinary vision in TA users for the reduction of losses and management of risks due to natural hazards in general. ERIES responds to the call INFRA-2021-SERV-01-07: Research infrastructures advancing frontier knowledge by: ● Providing TA to a plethora of leading RIs worldwide to European researchers in a simple and expeditious manner through the TA selection and evaluation panel (TA-SEP) composed of renowned experts. ● Possessing a core objective to promote cutting-edge research and develop innovative solutions in several Research Areas (RAs) to tomorrow’s problems via TA to world-renowned and unique RIs for earthquake, wind, geotechnical and structural interaction problems. ● Promoting the development and testing of new technologies and methodologies to improve the design against multi-hazards, to reduce damage and economic losses, to improve risk quantification and provide guidance on how to prioritize such risks in order to foster a safer, more resilient and greener society. ● Promote cross disciplinary fertilisations by addressing several hazards and seek ways to exploit existing knowledge and collaborate towards common research goals. ● Provide education and training activities for the TA users to foster a new generation of researchers equipped with knowledge on advanced experimental techniques regardless of their origin. ● Create a more harmonised and integrated network of RIs in Europe through the provision of TA focusing on geographically distributed hybrid simulation. (b) Ambition ERIES will achieve measurable progress beyond the state-of-the-art in the following areas: Progress in accessing experimental facilities for earthquake engineering Large scale experimental facilities in Europe may be regarded as some of the most advanced RIs for earthquake engineering worldwide, providing key data and experimental results for the development of new knowledge and innovative technologies aimed at improving the design standards and reducing the vulnerability of buildings and infrastructures to earthquake shaking. In the past, EU-funded projects such as NERA, SERIES and SERA have successfully provided integrated TA to some of these facilities. In ERIES, WP4-WP15 will offer integrated access to laboratories with high-class facilities in Europe. These may include the use of reaction walls, multi-directional shake table testing of full-scale houses using TA4 or TA5, for example, or industrial and non-structural components at the innovative 9 degree of freedom (DOF) shaking table (9DLAB) at TA1 in ways not previously possible. This 9DLAB installation in TA1 is the only of its kind in the world. TA3 will offer access to a unique worldwide field experimental facility comprising a whole instrumented valley with a permanent 3D accelerometric array and a large-scale testing facility of a model structure specifically designed to conduct SSI and wave propagation research in addition to research activities related to ground motion, site effects and engineering seismology. This experimental facility may be well combined with TA1 which offers access to MOBILAB, a mobile laboratory shaking unit that can replicate a powerful shaking table and also reproduce soil liquefaction and study seismic wave propagation including SSI effects 101058684 ERIES – Part B – 6 directly in-situ. So, the ERIES experimental facilities cover a wide spectrum of possible RIs in earthquake engineering to conduct high level innovative research on geohazards. These facilities, which represent 7 of the laboratories included in the ERIES project (TA1-TA7), therefore offer ample opportunity for European researchers to access and conduct their curiosity-driven research projects under the broader research goal of loss-driven approach, risk quantification and prioritization and the development of green and sustainable solutions to the problems investigated. Progress in accessing experimental facilities for wind engineering Regarding wind engineering research, the European experimental facilities for civil and environmental tests have a strong tradition related to classical boundary layer wind tunnel (BLWT) tests, capable of reproducing the effects of synoptic extra-tropical cyclonic wind on structures and structural elements, as well as on the built environment. They serve a wide range of public and private companies and furnish great impact on theoretical and industrial research and engineering standard sector. On the other hand, European facilities are lacking in some aspects, such as the absence of BLWT facilities in many countries prone to wind storms, the absence of advanced facilities for simulation of non-synoptic wind phenomena and the lack of integrated laboratory and free field experimental facilities. ERIES involves four major research organisations in Europe and North America, offering some of the most advanced facilities for wind engineering worldwide. These include two BLWTs (TA8 and TA10) used to assess wind loads on structures, structural elements and complex environments from small scale experiments; two large-scale BLWTs equipped with climate control capacities (TA11) which make it possible to assess the impact of wind alone or the combined effects of wind and other climatic parameters (i.e., temperature, solar radiation, rain, snow) on full-scale construction elements; two worldwide unique advanced facilities in TA9 capable of replicating large scale synoptic and non-synoptic winds as well as their combination and their effects on the built environment and a facility able to simulate full-scale aerodynamic pressures experienced during severe storms on typical building; one advanced Doppler Wind Lidar system (TA8) for monitoring high resolution continuous free-field wind flow. The partners also offer their multidisciplinary background, in-depth skills in instrumentation, numerical modelling and materials testing to support the experiments. The proposed RIs can operate complementary, exploring different topics such as complementarity of testing scales, climatic simulations, wind-blown debris, on site testing, wind effects of different meteorological phenomena, offering ample opportunity for European researchers to access and conduct their curiosity-driven research projects. In addition, the influence of SSI effects and their interaction with wind hazards can also be examined through cross-disciplinary interaction with the structural and geotechnical oriented TA3 and TA6, for example. Progress in accessing RIs for geographically distributed hybrid simulation and in-situ testing In addition to the state-of-the-art RIs made available, further progress will be made via ERIES in the integrated and synergetic use of multiple European laboratories together in addition to the use of mobile testing facilities. That is, hybrid simulation as an experimental testing technique to be used in different laboratories will be developed in close collaboration between at least two RIs in TA12. This means that while each individual lab has the capacity to test large components of structures (e.g., a large bridge pier tested using a reaction wall), other laboratories can be linked up digitally to test other components of the same bridge structure (e.g., end abutment or other piers) to create a European-wide testing network operating simultaneously. This type of integrated approach has not been available in previous projects such as SERIES and SERA and has not been extensively tested or developed in the field of structural engineering in Europe to exhibit its full potential to date. Furthermore, the provision of the mobile testing facility MOBILAB in TA1 will also permit advanced research and possible synergies between different RIs and research disciplines given that relatively powerful testing capabilities are no longer restricted to the confines of a laboratory. Progress in accessing top international RIs Gaining access to international RIs has been possible for European researchers, typically requiring bilateral cooperation and rather cumbersome and expensive procedures, while in some cases, laboratories may not allow, for several reasons, external users. To amply exploit the possibility offered by this call, ERIES includes an international beneficiary offering access in TA9 to a RI from Canada, which was specifically encouraged in this call, and possesses facilities currently unavailable to European researchers. Progress in the development of experimental techniques Further to the development of a network array of large scale European RIs operating complementarily to test individual components of large structures and infrastructures via hybrid simulation, progress will also be made via ERIES in the development of experimental methods using more advanced laboratory equipment and in the education and training provided to new users. For example, the provision of the novel 9DLab in Italy at TA1 represents a unique and promising facility that offers a host of benefits over traditional facilities available around the world for testing in earthquake engineering. Given its unique nature and availability in ERIES for a variety of research projects, 101058684 ERIES – Part B – 7 techniques and standards for testing with this type of arrangement will be developed through the experimental activities conducted. Progress in the mitigation of risk in Europe Further to the development of a network array of large scale European RIs operating complementarily to test individual components of large structures and infrastructures via hybrid simulation, progress will also be made via ERIES in the development of experimental methods using more advanced laboratory equipment and in the education and training provided to new users. For example, the provision of the novel 9DLab in Italy at TA1 represents a unique and promising facility that offers a host of benefits over traditional facilities available around the world for testing in earthquake engineering. Similarly, EUROSEISTEST-EUROPROTEAS facility at TA3 represents a unique worldwide facility in earthquake engineering, soil dynamics, SSI and engineering seismology (http://euroseisdb.civil.auth.gr/). Given its unique nature and availability in ERIES for a variety of research projects, techniques and standards for testing and developing new low-cost measuring instruments as in TA3, with this type of arrangement will be developed through the experimental activities conducted. The soil-foundation-structure-interaction (SoFSI) facility in TA6 is the first of its kind (worldwide) offering unique capabilities for studying experimentally different soil-structure systems under static, cyclic, and dynamic loads. Progress in the development of loss-driven approaches With one of the main research goals of ERIES comprising loss-driven approaches to seismic, wind and geotechnical issues coupled with world-class facilities, progress in these approaches will also be made. This will be achieved through the individual research projects that will be carried out and their orientation towards how monetary losses can be minimised and managed through the experimental tests conducted. That is, projects will make a distinct effort to show and demonstrate how their individual laboratory activities will directly or indirectly contribute to the increase in knowledge regarding loss-driven approaches when applied to these hazards. In many instances, and particularly related to indirect losses and disruption, this will represent a ground-breaking and frontier topic to be addressed. Progress in the development of green and sustainable risk mitigation solutions While the core research activities of ERIES will be to examine and test different structural typologies and configurations via TA and contribute towards an increased knowledge in risk quantification and loss-driven approaches, there will also be a strong focus on green and sustainable solutions. That is, for many of the issues being faced and investigated via TA, different climate-adaptation, sustainable and resilient solutions will be investigated. For the research projects funded and granted access to the laboratories available through ERIES, these will also need to highlight how the solutions they are investigating contribute to a greener and more sustainable society. That is, the benefits of these solutions over traditional ones will be an important aspect to highlight and disseminate. Addressing societal needs The report "The Human Cost of Disasters 2000-2019", published on 12th October 2020 by the United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR) and by the Centre for research on the epidemiology of disasters (CRED) on the occasion of the International Day for Disaster Risk Reduction, confirms that meteorological events are now predominant among the disasters of the 21st century; the past 20 years have seen a staggering increase in climate disasters: "In the period from 2000 to 2019, there were 7,348 major catastrophic events that claimed 1.23 million lives, affecting 4.2 billion people with consequent global economic losses of about 2.97 trillion dollars - underlines the UNDDR - This is a strong increase compared to the previous twenty years. From 3,656 climate-related events (1980-1999) to 6,681 climate-related disasters in the period 2000-2019”. Also in the last 20 years, the number of major floods has more than doubled, from 1,389 to 3,254 (40% of total climate disasters), while the incidence of storms has grown from 1,457 to 2,034 (28%), followed by earthquakes (8%) and extreme temperatures (6%). The report also notes major increases for fires and other severe-impact weather events. There has also been an increase in geophysical events, including earthquakes and tsunamis, which have killed more people than any other natural hazard examined in the report. Civil society is therefore becoming increasingly aware of the risks associated with disasters due to natural hazards, demanding more and more responsibility and a response, both preventive and restorative, from governments. In this contest, ERIES respond to some clear societal needs by: • Increasing capacity to deal with these disasters for Governments; • Bolstering prevention measure, in order to save time, financial resources, and human lives; • Increasing awareness and knowledge among general public. (c) RI Integration 101058684 ERIES – Part B – 8 ERIES will provide an overarching RI service at a European and worldwide level by the wide range of first-class laboratories available to work in a complementary way to respond to important missing gaps relevant to a variety of structures and structural components exposed to the different hazards they are addressing. ERIES will contribute to this by exploiting the successful synergies developed during past EU funded projects to extend to a strong network of leading laboratories capable of conducting combined cutting-edge research on multiple hazards. Of note is the geographic distribution of the different laboratories, shown in Figure 1, which span 11 different laboratories from 8 different countries addressing distinct but interconnected hazards focusing on frontier and curiosity-driven research. Also of great importance when considering the integration of RIs will be ERIES’ objective of combining multiple laboratories testing facilities online through geographically distributed hybrid testing techniques spanning multiple European countries to create an overarching RI synergy of much greater capacity. ERIES will contribute to the structuring of the European Research Area through its multi-disciplinary and complementary services offered in different fields of research. This will build on past experiences in the respective fields gained through past EU funded projects and build towards a more integrated and synergetic approach to risk reduction with respect to natural hazards. The scope and overall research goal of ERIES focuses directly on the impacts of natural hazards in terms of economic losses, resilience, downtime, and also the sustainability of the solutions proposed and explored in general, which will feed into markets for new products and devices. Furthermore, as will be discussed later, Research Area 4 will also focus on providing TA to research groups interested in developing specialised devices and systems that would be expected to be relatively close to market release and implementation in practice. Whilst this is a clearly positive facet since it shows the immediate impact the ERIES research will have, this does not discount the attention given to basic and frontier knowledge research activities with TRL < 3, which also play an important role in the project. This fundamental research will not likely make it to marketable products during the lifetime of this project but will nonetheless set the groundwork for future products to further test and implement using ERIES research. 1.2 Methodology (a) Concepts Structural design concepts addressing seismic hazard At the beginning of the XX century, major earthquakes hit San Francisco (1906, Magnitude≈7.8) and Messina (1908, Magnitude≈7.5). The assessed losses were of the order of $400 million (around €7 billion today) and 600 million Italian Lire (around €2 billion today), respectively. The casualties were 3,000 in San Francisco and over 80,000 in 101058684 ERIES – Part B – 9 Messina. After the Messina earthquake, a number of documents of exceptional value were published (e.g., Ceradini et al., 1913), essentially establishing the basis of modern earthquake engineering and setting the fundamentals of seismic structural design by comparing acting inertia forces and resisting elastic forces. However, it was clearly stated by Panetti (1914): “the effects of earthquakes on structures are in fact a structural dynamics problem, which is much too complicated to address”. These concepts were revisited by many scientists (e.g., Housner (1984) keynote lecture at the Eighth World Conference of Earthquake Engineering), progressively shifting from resisting forces to ductility, energy and displacement demand and capacity (e.g., SEAOC, 1995), later elaborated and extended in FEMA 356 (2000) and labelled performance-based earthquake engineering (PBEE). However, most of those documents were essentially aimed at modelling and predicting the response of single bare structures, as opposed to complete constructions, which includes non-structural elements such as cladding, glazing, ceilings, equipment and other contents, in addition to its interaction and interconnectivity with other structures and potential for disruption. As a consequence, the fundamental “performance objective” remained to avoid structural collapse, assumed as the unique factor related to the overall resilience to seismic events. Unfortunately, the correlation between probability of collapse and expected economic loss (i.e., cost of repair, downtime, loss of production capacity, industrial competitiveness and societal disruption) has been proven to be rather weak. Today, more than a century after the Messina earthquake and following a large cohesive effort by the earthquake engineering community to tackle the problem, we may state that: the effects of earthquakes on structures are a structural dynamics problem, which can be appropriately addressed using advanced experimental testing and investigation at world-class facilities and also through the use of non-linear finite element models and complementary studies. However, the moderate (Magnitude 5.9) earthquake that hit northern Italy in 2012 had only 27 victims but losses of over €13 billion, which would grow to more than 1% of the Italian GDP if indirect consequences were to be included. Similar conclusions may be drawn considering a number of recent seismic events worldwide (e.g., McGavin and Patrucco, 1994; Filiatrault et al., 2001; Chock et al., 2006; Daniell et al., 2011; United Nations University, 2011; Munich Re, 2014). As a further confirmation, recent simulations of a repeat of the 1908 Messina earthquake today indicate casualties between 6,000 and 50,000 and economic losses between €1.5-10 billion (Teramo et al., 2008). Furthermore, there has been an increased drive towards including more probabilistic approaches in earthquake engineering to manage and mitigate seismic risk (e.g., Cornell and Krawinkler, 2000) in addition to the direct evaluation of monetary losses (e.g., FEMA, 2012). Seismic risk reduction strategies have been categorized as ‘soft’ and ‘hard’ (Freddi et al., 2021), with the former referring to strategies involving early warning systems along with disaster financing and parametric insurance, whereas the latter entails novel structural systems/devices for new structures and retrofitting of existing structures. Mathematical models and tools have been elaborated to: aid the computation of expected loss (e.g., the Risk Modeller’s Toolkit, Silva et al. (2017)); aid in the decision making process when selecting optimal intervention strategies when considering a host of decision variables such as cost, disruption, architectural as well as environmental impacts (e.g., Carofilis et al., 2021; Clemett et al., 2021); and also allow a more rational prioritisation of available resources for the interventions required nationally in critical infrastructures like essential buildings (e.g., Grant et al., 2012; Petruzzelli and Iervolino, 2021) and bridge infrastructures (e.g., Kilanitis and Sextos, 2019). This recent shift has allowed engineers to begin quantifying performance in a way that may be more meaningful to stakeholders, using terminology like expected annual loss, probability of collapse and expected downtime - parameters that are undoubtedly of use when addressing the different problems observed in past events. It also provides a variety of potential solutions that span different disciplines and philosophies of intervention (i.e., soft vs. hard). While these undoubtedly represent progress in the field in terms of developing new possible avenues, there is still a clear need to bring the field of seismic risk mitigation forward through further compounding research in these areas to avoid a repeat of the problems of the past and present. The correlation between good design practice and risk mitigation is insufficient and the earthquake-related scientific community’s attempt at ensuring a resilient society has so far failed. The reasons for such a failure are numerous and complex and represent the core activities of the ERIES project’s seismic research: 1. The objective of building codes at obtaining a minimum, rather than a prescribed, level of safety implies an enormous waste of the limited available resources. 2. The application of advanced technologies (e.g., supplemental damping and seismic isolation) is often employed when there is little need and motivation, and not when significant economic advantages will result. 3. Consideration of expected economic losses in design and assessment is insufficient, generally only stated and not implemented in a proper manner. 4. Cascading effects and system interaction is not included in design, resulting in enormous potential for indirect losses and overall disruption. 5. Many issues related to the performance of pertinent elements such as building non-structural elements or 101058684 ERIES – Part B – 10 infrastructures like bridge piers and abutments have not been explored in adequate detail, partly due to the lack of experimental infrastructures capable of exploring particular issues. 6. Ageing and deterioration of entire sectors of the world infrastructure heritage contributes to the earthquake challenge. The case of road networks is emblematic: if bridges are collapsing under gravity in Europe (e.g., 2019 Morandi bridge collapse in Genoa), what may happen in case of seismic action (Calvi et al., 2019)? 7. While more advanced probabilistic approaches to tackle issues related to seismic risk are available, there is often a lack of experimental evidence to support some of the principal assumptions required; in addition, much of the data needed to quantify coefficients and estimate uncertainties in different parameters are lacking sufficient experimental or empirical evidence. 8. The investigation of greener and more sustainable materials and solutions to contribute towards tackling climate change require in-depth research and development that is somewhat lacking. Structural design concepts addressing wind hazard Windstorms can lead to structural damage and can have large impacts on human health and on structures, as well as on transport and energy infrastructures. According to Munich RE's natural catastrophe loss database (NatCatSERVICE), storms were the costliest natural hazard in Europe between 1980 and 2015; they ranked second for overall losses and fourth in terms of the number of human casualties (EEA Report No 15/2017). Windstorms can range from large storms covering a substantial part of the continent to relatively small and localised events. Large synoptic phenomena are extratropical cyclones, prevalent in Europe, and tropical cyclones (or hurricanes) in American and Asian countries. In southern Europe, tropical-like cyclones are known to occur over the Mediterranean Sea; they share several features with tropical cyclones, including winds up to hurricane force and heavy precipitation. Local non-synoptic phenomena causing strong winds are mostly related to tornadoes and thunderstorms. Their formation takes place in atmospheric zones characterized by strong instability, in particular along the cold fronts of cyclonic formations. Among the wind events, tornadoes are the ones that reach the highest speed. Observations of windstorms location, frequency and intensity showed considerable variability across Europe during the 20th century. However, most studies agree that the risk of severe storms will increase in the future for the North Atlantic, as well as for northern, north-western and central Europe. It is largely agreed that the warming of Earth's surface favours convective activity at the base of thunderstorms and this is why their number is increasing. Studies from the ESSL project increasing intensity especially in Mediterranean regions (Púčik et al., 2017). Enhancing the research and knowledge in this field is a priority of European policy for climate change adaptation and disaster risk reduction objectives. This concern is particularly critical since the normative documents used in Europe for the design of structures with regard to wind risk (Eurocode EN 1991-1-4 and the national annexes) are based on climatic maps established from historical data. The rapidity of climate change observed today suggests that the 50-year return periods used as reference values for the dimensioning of structures are likely obsolete. Fortunately, the safety coefficients introduced in the calculations of structures prevent damage from appearing in greater numbers. However, it is not satisfactory to support the security of the design of an infrastructure on the safety coefficients without taking into account the reality of evolving climatic phenomena. Moreover, current codes assume synoptic winds and thus ignore important features of the resulting wind-field and structural response due to thunderstorm downbursts, tornadoes and intermediate events. However, the insurance loss data indicate that a significant proportion, even the majority, of windstorm losses worldwide is due to convective storms and the associated downbursts, tornadoes and hail. Both synoptic and convective wind-storms are expected to become more frequent and more severe in the future. This will increase the wind-storm related risks. Wind force causes extensive damage through the direct actions associated with the air speed field and the indirect actions due to the impact of debris. Facing to recurrent wind-induced disasters and collapses, the evolution of knowledge of the wind and of its effects on structures and infrastructures has developed through the centuries, as a synthesis of multidisciplinary researches in many basic fields, as meteorology, aerodynamics, fluid and structural mechanics (Solari, 2019). In 1961 Alan Garnett Davenport proposed the famous “Davenport chain” (Davenport, 1961), linking together the fundamentals of wind, aerodynamics and mechanics, originating the first unitary procedure to analyse wind actions and effects on structures coherent with synoptic-scale extra-tropical cyclones. This pioneering study represents the milestone of a discipline that developed rapidly, covering the topics of statistical analysis of design wind speed (e.g., Palutikof et al., 1999), building aerodynamics (e.g., Tanaka et al., 2013), dynamic response of structures to the turbulent wind (e.g., Piccardo and Solari, 2002) and aeroelastic phenomena, such as the vortex shedding (e.g., Sarpkaya, 2004), galloping (e.g., Luongo and Piccardo, 2005) and flutter (e.g., Gu et al., 2000), representing wind-structure interaction phenomena affecting slender structures that often stray into devastating forms of instability. Relevant research efforts have also been addressed to other wind-induced effects on natural and built environment, such as transport problems and diffusion of pollutants (e.g., Holmes, and Morawska, 2006), wind effects on vehicles, transport and energy infrastructures (e.g., Repetto et al., 2018), and pedestrian comfort. 101058684 ERIES – Part B – 11 A fundamental role in the development of all these research topics has been played by experimental techniques. Free field anemometric monitoring campaign and free field full-scale structural monitoring facilities furnishes precious data on wind field characteristics and induced loads and responses of structures (e.g., Lombardo et al., 2018). Wind tunnel facilities, developed first in aeronautical field, have been used since the end of nineteenth century to study the aerodynamic forces on scaled models of buildings (Aynsley et al., 1977), and later specialising on civil and environmental tests able to reproduce the atmospheric boundary layer in the test section (Cermak, 1981). Their role in quantifying the wind-induced forces and wind-structure interaction phenomena is still undeniable. Furthermore, the investigation of the influence of foundation flexibility and soil failure during strong windstorms is an area that requires further research, especially given the cascading effects such failures can have. Despite the effort carried out in Europe and worldwide, many issues remain still unresolved in wind-induced hazard, vulnerability and risk assessment, due to extreme complexity of both the meteorological phenomena originating wind flows and wind-structure interaction; to those issues the ERIES project’s wind research is addressed. In particular: 1. Despite critical wind hazard being mostly related to tropical-like cyclones and local non-synoptic phenomena, building codes still address wind loading and structural response to extra-tropical cyclones model. The modelling of tornadoes and thunderstorms and their impact on the built environment are still dominated by many uncertainties, by the lack of a shared model of this phenomenon and its loading of structures, and by the need for a framework in which wind loading due to synoptic and non-synoptic outflows are encapsulated (Solari, 2020). 2. Standards and codes do not explicitly quantify the structural performance, and research on wind induced expected economic losses and downtime are still fragmentary (e.g., Bezabeh et al., 2020). 3. Wind effects and coupled wind-rain-hails effects on non-structural components are not covered by standards and codes and have not been explored in adequate detail, even if they are responsible, directly or indirectly, for a large part of wind-induced damage in urban environments generating debris. 4. The continuous growth of slender and flexible structures, such as high-rise buildings, bridges and wind turbines, calls for advanced numerical and experimental techniques to prevent aeroelastic effects and instability (Flamand et al., 2016). 5. In recent years, increasingly specialised large-scale laboratories, able to reproduce large-scale unsteady flow conditions or non-synoptic wind flows, such as tornadoes and downbursts have been developed (Hanganet al., 2017). Although the investment in manpower and funding for their maintenance and operation is high, their interest in studying the vulnerability of real construction components has been demonstrated (Flamand et al., 2019). Unfortunately, few of them are in Europe. A step forward towards innovative experimental techniques in advanced and traditional BLWT facilities would be highly beneficial for the European economy and research. 6. The crucial issue of scaling between model experiments and full-scale conditions is still a critical topic in experimental activities related to non-stationary events such as thunderstorms (e.g., Xu and Hangan, 2008) 7. Multi-hazard risk assessment is an emerging issue in wind and earthquake engineering. Innovative experimental techniques with multidisciplinary approach can have a fundamental role in the development of knowledge in this field which represents a new challenge to improve the structural safety of strategic structures (e.g., power plants and transmission systems). 8. Effect of soil-structure interaction on wind-induced responses of sensitive structures and effects of wind-induced structural vibrations on the soil behaviour are critical issues that call for interdisciplinary approach and innovative experimental methodologies (e.g., Kang et al., 2021). 9. Green and sustainable development is a major objective of EU Policy. Climate changes boost industries and scientific institutions towards more sustainable and greener urban environments. From this point of view, research in wind engineering field is a key factor to enhance innovation in renewable energy exploitation, including large-size wind turbines, photovoltaic panels, offshore installations; to improve the natural ventilation in the urban areas (e.g., Barlow, 2013) and increase the pollutant dispersion where the majority of human activities take place; to protect the soil from wind erosion and contrast desertification effects. Concepts related to the role of geotechnical engineering and geohazards Geotechnical engineering and soil-structure interaction (SSI) are integral parts of earthquake and wind engineering in many respects. Both affect the design of structures and civil infrastructure. However, from the point of view of natural hazards, geohazards and their effects on the built environment may be seen as interdependent perils. For example, landslides triggered by earthquakes or/and rainfall should be considered a typical geohazard that might considerably impact structures and infrastructure. Likewise, seismically triggered liquefaction phenomena are typical examples of geohazards seriously affecting structural performance. In the same family of hazards, one may also include several issues related to wave propagation in complex geological media induced by multiple sources (e.g., earthquakes, direct wind loading, or operation of wind turbines, man-made vibrations such as blast and structural oscillations) often coupled with the site, basin and topography effects which may increase the damages as it has 101058684 ERIES – Part B – 12 been demonstrated in the city of Izmir in Turkey during the recent Samos, Aegean Sea M7.0 earthquake (Makra et al., 2021). Additional geohazards of interest in experimental research include scour, soil fatigue under cyclic loads, flooding behind earth retaining structures, consolidation settlements under spatially extended infrastructure systems (e.g., railways and highways), static soil liquefaction, and geoenvironmental problems. SSI is inherent in the static and dynamic response of structures founded on soft ground (FEMA, 2020; NIST, 2012). Owing to its complexity and unique characteristics (notably radiation damping) that do not exist in rigidly supported structures, SSI in earthquake and wind engineering is generally treated as an independent engineering problem. Despite systematic studies on SSI for almost a whole century, there is still controversy regarding its role in the performance of structures. In fact, excluding specific conditions (Stewart et al., 1999; Mylonakis et al., 2006), seismic SSI has traditionally been considered beneficial for seismic response (Ciampoli & Pinto, 1995; Mylonakis & Gazetas, 2000). As a result, SSI effects are generally neglected in design, assuming this leads to broader safety margins. However, this traditional perception, which is widespread both in research and practice, has significant implications in the design of new structures or assessing existing ones, including evaluating losses, risk, and resilience. SSI effects influence both the seismic demand and, in some instances, structural capacity. An overestimation of these two variables in the seismic assessment of existing structures may lead - at least in some cases - to expensive retrofit interventions that would not be necessary with a seismic assessment incorporating SSI effects. This statement has been documented in the seismic assessment of a variety of important structures, including bridges (Mylonakis et al., 2006; Sextos et al., 2003), offshore wind turbines (Ali et al., 2020), tunnels (Al Heib et al., 2020), monuments (Fiorentino et al., 2019), and concrete dams (Chopra, 2020). Recent research (Andreotti & Calvi, 2021) further shows that soil properties surrounding structural elements (e.g., piles) may affect structural capacity by altering the position, hierarchy, and degree of damage. This evidence has significant implications in the performance evaluation of new or existing structural elements subjected to lateral dynamic loading (e.g., earthquake or wind) or static loading (e.g., thermal action). Most importantly, aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty associated with SSI effects propagates into assessing seismic fragility, vulnerability, and risk of buildings (Petridis & Pitilakis, 2020), and often is the dominant factor for decision-making. However, due to the complex and multi-parameter nature of dynamic soil-structure interaction and the large dimensions of the problem, very few field-scale experimental SSI studies exist (Star et al., 2015, Pitilakis et al., 2018). In fact, most of the research findings are analytical or numerical in nature (Pitilakis et al., 2008), the latter often involving multi-platform simulation (Kwon & Elnashai, 2008) or coupled boundary and finite element solutions that successfully couple the far- and the near-field respectively. Large scale experimentation of the coupled SSI system (Spencer et al., 2006), including pseudo-dynamic sub-structuring and geographically distributed hybrid testing (Bousias et al., 2019), have further contributed in quantifying the importance of SSI and drawing the thresholds beyond which they need to be considered in single- or multi-hazard risk assessment. Finally, seismic and vibration isolation techniques are generally expensive to be widely used in practice, except for some specific high-importance structures and buildings. In this respect, research on the development of low-cost seismic and vibration isolation techniques, like for example the use as foundation of sand-gravel mixtures with recycled rubber material, known as Geotechnical Seismic Isolation system may be an attractive solution for wider application in practice responding at the same time at the green economy requirements. Notwithstanding the above advancements, the reliable experimental quantification of geohazard-triggered SSI effects is still a missing element for reducing the design conservatism associated with the high uncertainties involved. In this context, ERIES aims to offer TA to world-class facilities to conduct curiosity-driven, cutting-edge research in several topics related to geohazard, risk, and SSI effects, which remain poorly or not yet adequately explored. In particular: 1. The experimental investigation of the dynamic response of integral abutment bridges and interaction effects between backfill and stem of the abutment wall under cyclic loading (e.g., thermal action, earthquake loading) is still uncertain. This is a significant global problem, as integral bridges greatly simplify construction and lower maintenance costs. 2. The cyclic behaviour of foundations supporting wind turbines and their interaction with the soil has not been studied experimentally to a sufficient extent. The specific problem is of fundamental importance in geotechnics and soil-structure interaction, with widespread applications in wind farms, and may involve synergies with the wind group and groups specializing in structural control. The importance of combined wind and wave loading may be investigated in conjunction with the softening ("fatigue") of soil around the pile under cyclic loading, which often leads to permanent rotations of the pile head. The importance of structural control as a mitigating mechanism on the structural side can also be explored. The evaluation of the impact of oscillating turbines to neighbouring structures and buildings is still unknown and necessitate specific field experiments of wave propagation and validation. 3. The low-cost, environmentally conscious seismic isolation systems for developed as well as for low-income countries are becoming prevalent. The use of low-cost recycled material in seismic isolation has been a subject of investigation during the last two decades, mostly in laboratory and small-scale laboratory specimen 101058684 ERIES – Part B – 13 tests (Senetakis et al., 2012). Numerical analyses shed light on the potential use of such concepts in seismic isolation of structures (Tsang et al., 2008), whereas recently, field-scale (Pitilakis et al., 2021) and shaking table tests (Tsiavos et al., 2021) sparked the interest in actual applications of Geotechnical Seismic Isolation (GSI) systems. Nevertheless, the optimal configuration of such systems is yet to be scrutinized to be used as innovative vibration isolation against high-frequency (for example, high-speed rail) and low-frequency signals (i.e., small to medium intensity earthquakes). 4. The dynamic response of structural and non-structural elements might be affected by SSI. Experimental study of the dynamic response of structural and non-structural elements on small- and large-scale under soil-borne excitations may elucidate such effects. These tests can be performed using the GSI systems as well. 5. Specific code-oriented methods for spatial variability of ground motion, soil and site effects in complex geological media including basin, valley and azimuth effects are still missing comprehensive experimental validation. 6. The static and dynamic pile group effects, including dynamic p-y curves and corresponding pile group multipliers, are still missing experimental validation. This represents one of the most important unsolved problems in geotechnical engineering and SSI, mainly due to the difficulty (and cost) of testing pile groups at/near full scale. The importance of key parameters such as pile diameter, spacing, length, and fixity conditions at the head can be investigated in the ERIES facilities. 7. Small-scale experiments on earthquake-induced liquefaction are sparse, and field-scale tests are practically non-existent. The ERIES installations could lead the research in this direction, in small- and field-scale, with well-controlled testing of induced soil liquefaction, to study its effects on structural response. 8. The effect of structural rocking on compliant soil (Anastasopoulos et al., 2010) under far- and near-fault ground shaking has not been studied experimentally. The solution to this complex problem has great implications in seismic design, as rocking is known to have beneficial effects on seismic response (Vassiliou et al., 2020). Nevertheless, the complexity of seismic rocking under rigid base conditions and the (practically unavoidable) large soil settlements under soft soil conditions have prevented using "rocking-oriented" seismic design in practice. The effects of soil compliance can be explored parametrically on the ERIES installations. Effects of scaling, number of blocks, boundary conditions, and interface action can be investigated. 9. Research on ERIES installations could tackle fundamental problems such as experimental investigation of time-evolving properties of civil infrastructure (e.g., wind turbines, bridges crossing rivers) due to scouring and/or cyclic fatigue. 10. In addition, the development of innovative low-cost sensors (MEM) for seismic monitoring (free field and structural) and their calibration requires extensive experimental testing, which can be carried out on ERIES installations. 11. Finally, a combination of the ERIES infrastructure offers a well-suited option for pseudo-dynamic, geographically distributed, hybrid simulation for rate-independent soil-structure interaction problems (see dedicated WP15). Overview Considering the above issues raised regarding seismic, wind and geo-related hazards, and also considering their pertinence to the ‘Geosphere’ scientific domain called as part of the INFRA-2021-SERV-01-07 call, ERIES identifies three specific research goals and areas of research within which to focus its experimental activities, shown in Figure 2. The research goals essentially reflect the technical aspects of the specific objectives outlined in Section 1.1. It has been broken down into specific areas that can be studied for all hazards and will be specifically mentioned in the calls for proposals made for the TA user groups. In particular, they relate to: ● Research Area 1 (RA1): Built Environment - This encompasses the structures in which we live and occupy each day. They are analysed from a single-structure perspective in relation to their associated attributes; these cover the building typology (e.g., reinforced concrete, steel, unreinforced masonry), the occupancy type (e.g., residential, commercial, office) and other pertinent issues like the non-structural elements and building contents. ● Research Area 2 (RA2): Critical Infrastructures - This encompasses the structures which form a more strategic and regional part of daily life. They are analysed singularly but with a view to encompassing their cascading effects on the networks to which they belong. Road, rail, water, gas or electric networks and other infrastructures like wind turbines and power plants are the main focus, which can then be subdivided based on more detailed attributes like typology (e.g., bridge, transmission line, concrete, steel), structural system (e.g., simply supported, arched, precast) in addition to the study of geotechnical issues and supplemental device behaviour (see also RA4). ● Research Area 3 (RA3): Industrial Facilities - This encompasses the structures which form the basis of much industrial activity and whose characteristics and needs do not necessarily fall under RA1. In particular, 101058684 ERIES – Part B – 14 industrial facilities like factories and production plants, which play a crucial role in the economic functionality of a region, are dealt with in a more specific manner. Issues like the structural performance of the unique structures that house such industrial production will be examined, in addition to the non-structural elements (see also RA1) and other components that typically occupy these facilities. ● Research Area 4 (RA4): Advanced Technologies - Differing from the previous three RAs, this research area focuses on the advancement of technologies. This is done first from a mitigation devices perspective, whereby existing technologies such as base isolation, viscous dampers and other innovative green and sustainable materials are developed and can be readily implemented in practice. It is envisioned that their development can also produce an indirect impact on RAs 1-3 previously described. Secondly, this research area focuses on experimental techniques as a topic to be further developed. That is, the capacity of the different RIs to develop and expand their capabilities, with a focus on the different partners’ laboratories working together in a novel form of hybrid testing, will be given adequate consideration. Considering these four RAs and the hazards to be examined under the umbrella of the overall research goal, the following sections will link the concepts outlined with more practical steps that may be taken through ERIES project activities to address these current research gaps.
